title: Logarithm (Full Elite 30Q)
subject: math
summary: Logarithms reverse exponents and are highly useful in algebraic simplification and equation solving.
SPTG often uses logs in a practical and pattern-based way, while harder variants may involve change of base, equations, identities, and exponential-log interplay.
The key is to understand logs conceptually, not just memorize rules.
coreConcepts: A logarithm tells the exponent needed to turn the base into the argument.
Product, quotient, and power rules come directly from exponent laws.
Change of base allows logs with different bases to be rewritten in a common base.
Domain matters: the argument of a logarithm must be positive.
The base must be positive and not equal to 1.
Equal logs with the same base imply equal arguments, provided the expressions are valid.
Many log equations become easier after converting them into exponential form.
Some questions mix logs and exponents and require switching forms flexibly.
SPTG-style traps often include illegal splitting such as log(a+b) ≠ log a + log b.
derive: Definition: log_a(b) = x if and only if a^x = b.
If x = log_a(M) and y = log_a(N), then M = a^x and N = a^y.
So MN = a^x · a^y = a^(x+y), hence log_a(MN) = x + y = log_a(M) + log_a(N).
Similarly, M/N = a^(x-y), so log_a(M/N) = log_a(M) - log_a(N).
Also, M^k = (a^x)^k = a^(kx), so log_a(M^k) = k log_a(M).
For change of base, let x = log_a(b), so a^x = b. Taking log_c of both sides gives x log_c(a) = log_c(b), hence x = log_c(b) / log_c(a).
If log_a(f(x)) = log_a(g(x)), then f(x) = g(x), provided both arguments are positive.
Many equations simplify by collecting terms into a single logarithm and then converting back to exponential form.
formulas: If log_a(f(x)) = log_a(g(x)), then f(x) = g(x), with valid domains
Argument of any logarithm must be > 0
Base must satisfy a > 0 and a ≠ 1
traps: log(a+b) is NOT equal to log a + log b.
log(a-b) is NOT equal to log a - log b.
When solving log equations, always check the domain at the end.
Changing base incorrectly is a common mistake; log_a(b) = log(b)/log(a), not the other way around.
If bases differ, combine carefully or rewrite before comparing.
A candidate solution may satisfy the algebra but fail the positivity condition inside a log.
In equations like log_x(16) = 2, the base x must be positive and not equal to 1.
When simplifying powers, keep track of whether the coefficient multiplies the whole log or only part of the expression.

pat1Set.question: If log_a(b) = m and log_a(c) = n, express log_a(b^2 / c^3).
